planting.note: Divide root suckers or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; staghorn sumac suckers aggressively — plant where spreading can be tolerated or mow around the perimeter annually to contain the colony.

directions: Divide root suckers or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; staghorn sumac is a fast-growing, large native shrub or small tree that provides one of the most spectacular multi-season landscape performances of any woody plant: bold, tropical-looking, compound leaves with 11–31 leaflets create a dramatic summer canopy; brilliant, fire-engine-red to orange-scarlet fall foliage among the most vivid of any temperate tree or shrub; fuzzy, cone-shaped clusters of deep-red, acidic berries (drupes) that persist through winter and provide critical food for ruffed grouse, robins, thrushes, bluebirds, pheasants, wild turkeys, and numerous other birds; and the distinctive, densely furry, brown-velvet branch tips that resemble the antlers of a stag in velvet — the most distinctive structural feature of any dormant shrub. Berries are used to make a pleasantly tart, pink lemonade-like beverage (sumac-ade) in indigenous and folk traditions. 'Laciniata' (cut-leaf/dissected foliage) is a particularly elegant ornamental cultivar. The suckering habit makes it excellent for erosion control on slopes.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; purple beautyberry benefits from hard pruning to 6–12 inches in late winter each year to encourage vigorous new growth and the heaviest berry production.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; purple beautyberry is an Asian relative of the native American beautyberry (Callicarpa americana) that produces even more concentrated and numerous berry clusters — the tightly packed, intensely violet-purple berries appearing in dense whorled clusters along the arching stems from late summer through early winter, creating one of the most remarkable late-season displays in the shrub border. The berries are small (about the size of a peppercorn) but clustered in such profuse, beaded rings that the effect is extraordinary — appearing almost artificially vivid. The naturally arching growth habit creates an elegant silhouette in all seasons. Excellent combined with ornamental grasses and fall-blooming asters. Prune hard to 6–12 inches in late winter annually to maximize berry production on current-season wood. 'Early Amethyst' (bears fruit weeks earlier than the species), 'Issai' (compact, very heavy-bearing), and 'Albifructus' (white berries) are notable cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide established suckers or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to alkaline, average to poor soil in partial to full shade in spring; coralberry is the most shade and drought tolerant of the native fruiting shrubs and excels in the deep dry shade under dense tree canopy.

directions: Divide suckers or plant container specimens in well-drained to dry, average to poor soil in partial to full shade; coralberry (Indian currant, buckbrush) is one of the most valuable and underused native shrubs for the most difficult landscape challenge: dry, deep shade under dense tree canopy where most plants refuse to grow. Coralberry not only survives in these conditions but thrives, spreading steadily by suckers to form colonies of arching, wiry stems bearing small, oval, bluish-green leaves and dense clusters of small, coral-pink to rosy-red berries that persist through winter — important food for bobwhite, pheasant, grouse, cedar waxwings, and other wildlife. Unlike most native shrubs, it tolerates alkaline soil, clay, and root competition from established trees. Its stoloniferous spreading habit makes it excellent for erosion control on shaded slopes and embankments. The cultivar 'Foliis Variegatis' has yellow-variegated leaves that are effective in lightening dark shade. Often overlooked by gardeners in favor of showier shrubs, it is irreplaceable for solving the deep-dry-shade problem.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, sandy soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; northern bayberry fixes nitrogen through root associations with Frankia bacteria and thrives in infertile, sandy, coastal soils — fertilizing is counterproductive and produces excessive, rank growth.

directions: Plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; northern bayberry is a native, semi-evergreen shrub of eastern North American coastal areas that provides four compelling reasons for wider use: extraordinary tolerance of sandy, nutrient-poor, salty coastal soil where most shrubs fail; dense clusters of small, waxy, blue-gray berries along the stems that provide essential late-autumn and winter food for myrtle warblers (yellow-rumped warblers), tree swallows, and other migratory birds that depend on the high-fat bayberry wax for energy during migration; the rich, resinous, spicy-sweet fragrance of the leaves and berries that perfumes the landscape in warm weather; and the historical use of the waxy berries in traditional bayberry candle-making. Dioecious — plant both male and female plants for berry production. Nitrogen-fixing root associations with Frankia bacteria allow the plant to thrive in infertile soils without fertilization.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide established root suckers in early spring or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy to gravelly, infertile soil in full sun to partial shade; sweetfern resents fertile soil and will not thrive in amended or enriched garden soil — this is a plant specifically for lean, acidic, infertile conditions.

directions: Divide root suckers in early spring or plant container specimens in well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy, infertile soil in full sun to partial shade; sweetfern is one of the most distinctive and characterful of all North American native shrubs — though it looks like a miniature fern with deeply lobed, linear, fern-like leaves, it is actually a nitrogen-fixing, aromatic shrub related to bayberry (Morella), not a fern at all. The leaves have a remarkable, intensely spicy-sweet, resinous fragrance when bruised or crushed — one of the most evocative natural scents of the northeastern American outdoors, instantly recognizable to anyone who has walked through sunny pine barrens, rocky slopes, and sandy blueberry barrens in summer. It thrives in extremely infertile, dry, sandy, acidic soils where virtually nothing else will grow, and is essential for naturalizing and stabilizing infertile, acidic slopes and disturbed areas. The nitrogen-fixing root system slowly enriches the surrounding soil, making it useful as a nurse plant for subsequent native plantings. Essentially impossible to transplant bare-root; always use container stock.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
